Keywords: purple gradient, indigo gradient, AI gradient, generic SaaS
Why It's Bad: Overused 2020-2025 across every AI startup; signals 'generic AI aesthetic' and erases brand identity
How to Detect: linear-gradient with purple/indigo + blue/cyan stops; #6366f1 → #8b5cf6 → #3b82f6 patterns
Better Alternative: Single dominant brand color with one sharp accent; or a deliberately unexpected palette (warm earth, monochrome + neon, duotone)

Keywords: gradient text, background-clip text, rainbow text
Why It's Bad: Reduces contrast, often fails WCAG AA, dates the design to the 2022 SaaS era
How to Detect: background-clip: text on h1/h2/h3
Better Alternative: Solid color with strong type weight; reserve gradients for ornamental marks only

Keywords: low contrast, gray on gray, accessibility, wcag fail
Why It's Bad: Fails WCAG AA (4.5:1); excludes low-vision users; signals lazy design
How to Detect: Foreground / background contrast ratio < 4.5:1 for body, < 3:1 for large text
Better Alternative: Test with axe-core or WebAIM contrast checker; raise foreground or darken background until ≥ 4.5:1

Keywords: Inter font, default font, generic sans, system font
Why It's Bad: Most-used webfont 2022-2025; instantly signals 'I used the default'; zero brand differentiation
How to Detect: font-family: 'Inter' on body without an intentional pairing
Better Alternative: Pair Inter with a distinctive display font (Fraunces, Instrument Serif, Editorial New); or pick a different sans entirely (Geist, Söhne, Untitled Sans, Aeonik)

Anti-Pattern: Geist / Mona Sans / Space Grotesk reflex
Keywords: Geist, Mona Sans, Space Grotesk, overused font
Why It's Bad: 2024-2025 default for every Next.js/Vercel-adjacent project; commoditized
How to Detect: font-family includes Geist, Mona Sans, or Space Grotesk without a distinctive pairing
Better Alternative: Use a less-saturated alternative: Söhne, Aeonik, Basier, Suisse, GT America, or a free Google Font like Manrope/DM Sans/Onest

Keywords: Fraunces body, serif body overuse
Why It's Bad: Fraunces is a display face; using it for body kills readability and is a 2024 cliché
How to Detect: font-family on p/li/body includes Fraunces
Better Alternative: Keep Fraunces for display headlines only; pair with a clean sans or geometric serif for body

Keywords: nested card, card-in-card, layered card, russian doll card
Why It's Bad: Visual noise; redundant elevation; signals 'I wrapped everything in shadcn Card'
How to Detect: Card component containing another Card with its own border/shadow
Better Alternative: Flatten: use one card with internal dividers, or remove the inner card entirely

Keywords: left border accent, side stripe, accent bar, ribbon border
Why It's Bad: Tutorial-blog cliché 2018-2024; reads as decorative not informative
How to Detect: border-left: 4px solid <accent> on Card or Alert without semantic meaning
Better Alternative: Use a meaningful icon, a tag, or solid background tint to signal status — don't decorate

Keywords: thick border, accent card, decorative border
Why It's Bad: Adds weight without information; usually masks a layout that lacks hierarchy
How to Detect: border: 3px+ on container with bright color and no semantic meaning
Better Alternative: Increase typographic hierarchy and spacing instead; reserve thick borders for genuinely active or selected states

Keywords: generic landing, default landing, AI landing page, hero + 3 columns
Why It's Bad: The 'AI landing page' shape: hero → 3-feature grid → testimonials → pricing → CTA. Looks identical to 10,000 other sites
How to Detect: Section order matches the boilerplate without intentional deviation
Better Alternative: Break the grid: asymmetric hero, marquee, scroll-triggered story, an unusual section ordering, or a single-page manifesto layout

Keywords: tight spacing, cramped, no breathing room, dense
Why It's Bad: Sub-32px section padding on landing pages; everything feels rushed; reads as low-budget
How to Detect: section padding < 64px on desktop, < 32px on mobile
Better Alternative: Use 96-160px section padding on desktop, 48-80px on mobile; let content breathe

Keywords: over-animated, motion overload, fade-in everything
Why It's Bad: Every element fades/slides in on scroll; cumulative effect feels chaotic and slow
How to Detect: scroll-triggered animation on > 30% of components per page
Better Alternative: Reserve motion for hero entry, key reveals, and interaction feedback; static is fine

Keywords: static design, no animation, lifeless
Why It's Bad: On a brand or marketing page, total absence of motion reads as cheap or unfinished
How to Detect: Zero transition, zero hover effect, zero scroll reveal
Better Alternative: Add at least: button hover (150-200ms), one orchestrated hero entrance, one delight micro-interaction

Keywords: default transition, generic easing, ease-in-out everywhere
Why It's Bad: Browser-default cubic-bezier reads as untuned; nothing feels considered
How to Detect: transition: all 300ms ease on every interactive element
Better Alternative: Use intentional curves: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) for entries, cubic-bezier(0.7, 0, 0.84, 0) for exits; vary duration 120-450ms by purpose

Keywords: default shadcn, untouched components, factory shadcn
Why It's Bad: Out-of-the-box shadcn looks identical across thousands of sites; commoditizes the brand
How to Detect: Components match shadcn defaults: same radius, same border, same shadow, same neutral
Better Alternative: Theme aggressively: change radius, border weight, shadow language, font, accent — make it yours

Keywords: slate-500, gray-500, zinc-500, default tailwind
Why It's Bad: Slate/zinc/gray defaults read as 'I didn't pick a palette'
How to Detect: Foreground or background uses default Tailwind gray scale without semantic mapping
Better Alternative: Define a semantic palette in CSS variables (--bg, --fg, --muted, --accent) and never reference raw Tailwind grays in components

Anti-Pattern: Stock photo of laptop on white desk
Keywords: stock photo, generic stock, laptop on desk, handshake photo
Why It's Bad: Signals zero art direction; the visual equivalent of lorem ipsum
How to Detect: Hero image is a generic stock photo (Unsplash top results, Shutterstock filler)
Better Alternative: Custom illustration, real product UI, branded photography, or a strong typographic hero with no image at all

Keywords: 3D blob, gradient blob, abstract shape, generic illustration
Why It's Bad: Used by every Web3/SaaS/AI site 2021-2024; reads as filler
How to Detect: SVG with random bezier curves and a purple/blue gradient fill
Better Alternative: Branded illustration, isometric line art, real product screenshots, or a dramatic typographic statement

Keywords: fake dashboard, mockup screenshot, dummy data
Why It's Bad: Numbers don't tell a story; readers smell that it's fake
How to Detect: Dashboard hero with charts that show no clear trend or insight
Better Alternative: Use real anonymized data with a clear narrative; or stylize so heavily it's clearly illustrative

Keywords: we believe, we're on a mission, change the world
Why It's Bad: Generic mission-statement copy that any company could publish
How to Detect: Hero or About section uses 'we believe', 'we're on a mission', 'change the world', 'reimagine'
Better Alternative: Lead with a specific user problem and a specific outcome; cite a concrete number or moment

Anti-Pattern: AI assistant tone in product UI
Keywords: as an AI, I'd love to help, certainly!, I understand
Why It's Bad: Reads as ChatGPT-flavored; sterile, sycophantic, untrustworthy
How to Detect: Strings include 'as an AI', 'I'd love to help', 'Certainly!', 'I understand', 'Great question'
Better Alternative: Plain direct voice: 'Done.', 'Try this.', 'Here it is.' Match the user's register

Keywords: missing focus, no outline, focus invisible, keyboard nav
Why It's Bad: Keyboard users can't see where they are; WCAG 2.1.1 / 2.4.7 fail
How to Detect: outline: none on :focus without :focus-visible replacement
Better Alternative: Provide a clear :focus-visible ring (≥ 2px, ≥ 3:1 contrast), distinct from hover

Keywords: div onclick, div click handler, no semantic
Why It's Bad: Not focusable, not announced to screen readers, not keyboard-accessible
How to Detect: <div onClick=...> or <span onClick=...> without role/tabindex/keyboard
Better Alternative: Use a real <button> or <a>; if you must use div, add role='button', tabindex='0', and keyboard handlers

Keywords: placeholder label, no label, missing label
Why It's Bad: Disappears on input; fails screen readers; bad UX for everyone
How to Detect: <input placeholder='Email'> with no associated <label>
Better Alternative: Visible label above or floating; placeholder for example/format hint only

Keywords: small button, tiny tap target, finger size
Why It's Bad: Buttons < 44×44px on mobile fail Apple HIG and WCAG 2.5.5
How to Detect: Interactive element with bounding box < 44px on touch
Better Alternative: Minimum 44×44px (iOS) or 48×48dp (Android); generous padding even when icon-only

Keywords: animating width, animating height, layout thrash
Why It's Bad: Triggers layout/paint on every frame; janky on low-end devices
How to Detect: CSS transition includes width, height, top, left, margin
Better Alternative: Animate transform and opacity only; use will-change sparingly

Keywords: unoptimized image, no webp, no avif, large hero
Why It's Bad: 6MB hero PNG; LCP > 4s; mobile users bounce
How to Detect: Hero serves uncompressed PNG/JPG without responsive srcset
Better Alternative: Serve AVIF/WebP with PNG fallback, responsive srcset, width/height attrs to prevent CLS

Keywords: logo crowded, logo no clear space, logo squeezed
Why It's Bad: Logo placed flush against text or other elements; brand looks careless
How to Detect: Logo bounding box has < 1× cap-height of clear space on all sides
Better Alternative: Define and enforce minimum clear space = height of one letterform on all four sides

Keywords: logo lockup confusion, multiple wordmarks, logo soup
Why It's Bad: Header has wordmark, favicon, and a third icon — all different
How to Detect: Page renders 2+ visually distinct brand marks
Better Alternative: Pick one primary lockup; secondary marks (favicon, app icon) should be derivatives, not redesigns

Keywords: all centered, lazy alignment, no hierarchy
Why It's Bad: Everything centered = no hierarchy; eye has nowhere to land
How to Detect: Hero, sections, and cards all use text-align: center
Better Alternative: Use left-align for body, reserve center for short statements; combine with intentional asymmetry
